name: Ekkeko
description: Ekkeko is a deity of prosperity, abundance, and good fortune in Incan and Andean mythology. He is often depicted as a small, jovial man with a mustache, laden with bags of goods, representing all the things one might desire: food, money, tools, and even houses. He is a symbol of happiness and wish fulfillment, granting the desires of those who venerate him. Ekkeko is believed to bring wealth and abundance to the household where he resides. Traditionally, a miniature of the desired item is attached to the Ekkeko figurine, acting as a symbolic petition. While his exact origins are shrouded in pre-Incan history, he is associated with the Tiwanaku culture and was later adopted into the Incan pantheon. The deity's popularity persists strongly in modern-day Bolivia and Peru, where he is a central figure in the Alasitas fair, a festival dedicated to miniature representations of desired objects, offered to Ekkeko in hopes of their materialization. He is not a demanding god, but rather a benevolent figure who appreciates simple offerings such as cigarettes, alcohol, and small tokens of gratitude. Though not part of the core Incan state religion, Ekkeko holds significant importance in the daily lives of many Andean people, representing hope and the possibility of a better future. His power resides in his ability to manifest desires and bring prosperity to those who believe in him. The more items he carries, the more potent his blessings are believed to be. Ekkeko's continued worship demonstrates the enduring appeal of a deity focused on personal well-being and the fulfillment of earthly desires.
subtitle: God of prosperity, abundance, and fortune.

keyMyths.title: Ekkeko and the Drought
keyMyths.description: A tale tells of Ekkeko bringing rain and abundance to a drought-stricken village, proving his power to provide. His presence restored the land's fertility and brought prosperity back to the people.

keyMyths.title: The Origins of Alasitas
keyMyths.description: The Alasitas fair originated as a celebration honoring Ekkeko, where people offer miniature representations of their desires. It is believed that Ekkeko will grant these wishes if the offerings are made with faith and respect.

keyMyths.title: Ekkeko's Humility
keyMyths.description: Despite his power, Ekkeko is said to appreciate simple offerings like cigarettes and alcohol. This highlights his accessibility and connection to the common people, rather than demanding lavish tributes.

sources.author: Alan R. Sandstrom
